chunks.text: Each year, top students from across the world are attracted to our undergraduate programs by the excellent faculty, the breadth of educational opportunities, the proximity to the vibrant California high-tech economy, and the Berkeley environment. Our close ties to industry, coupled with our commitment to research and education, ensure that students get a rigorous, relevant, and broad education.
chunks.question: What attracts students to Berkeley's EECS undergraduate programs?
chunks.answer: Excellent faculty, opportunities, and industry ties.

chunks.text: The Electrical Engineering & Computer Sciences Department offers three different undergraduate degrees from two different colleges: the Bachelor of Science in Electrical Engineering & Computer Sciences (EECS) and the Bachelor of Science in Electrical & Computer Engineering, both offered by the College of Engineering (CoE), and the Bachelor of Arts in Computer Science (CS) from the College of Computing, Data Science, and Society (CDSS). Technical requirements in computer science are almost identical; however, breadth requirements and admissions processes vary significantly.
chunks.question: What are the three undergraduate degrees offered by the EECS department?
chunks.answer: EECS BS, ECE BS, and CS BA.

chunks.text: Computer Science deals with computer theory and methods of information processing, hardware & software design, and applications. The major combines a rigorous technical program with background in the liberal arts and sciences.
chunks.question: What does the Computer Science major at Berkeley combine?
chunks.answer: Technical rigor with liberal arts background.

chunks.text: The CoE Electrical Engineering & Computer Sciences major combines fundamentals of computer science and electrical engineering in one major.
chunks.question: What does the EECS BS major combine?
chunks.answer: Computer science and electrical engineering fundamentals.

chunks.text: Electrical and Computer Engineering plays a crucial role in technological advancements across interdisciplinary areas that include neurotechnology, integrated circuits, microelectronics, nanotechnology, computer architecture, sensing and communication, robotics and sustainable energy systems.
chunks.question: What areas does Electrical and Computer Engineering impact?
chunks.answer: Neurotechnology, robotics, and sustainable energy systems.

chunks.text: The CS Major offers greater flexibility to explore other fields, such as economics, music, etc., with breadth coursework. If you are more interested in engineering, the EECS or ECE Majors may be better suited to you.
chunks.question: Which major offers more flexibility for exploring other fields?

chunks.text: There are many reasons to think seriously about UC Berkeley. It is one of the most distinguished institutions of higher learning, with a leading EECS department, a world-renowned faculty, a strong commitment to excellence in undergraduate education, and a beautiful campus situated opposite the Golden Gate Bridge near the San Francisco Bay. We are also a short distance from Silicon Valley, and a number of high-tech companies are also based in the Berkeley and Alameda areas. This close proximity to the latest technologies enlivens our curriculum, provides many research and summer job opportunities for our students, and makes this a very exciting place to study electrical engineering and computer sciences.
chunks.question: Why is UC Berkeley a distinguished choice for EECS studies?
chunks.answer: Leading department, renowned faculty, and Silicon Valley proximity.

chunks.text: There is, and will continue to be, a high demand for EE and CS engineers. Due to the rapid pace of change, Berkeley’s academic program is flexible and emphasizes fundamentals. You will use up-to-date undergraduate computer and laboratory facilities. Distinguished teachers from the Berkeley campus will be your lecturers, advisors, and mentors. You can participate in undergraduate research projects. Engineers usually work in teams, so we also encourage our students to take courses to sharpen their communication skills.
chunks.question: What does Berkeley's EECS program emphasize due to rapid technological change?

chunks.text: Participation in a summer research project can provide you with valuable new skills and experiences to use on your résumé.
chunks.question: What benefit does summer research offer to students?
chunks.answer: Valuable skills and experiences for resumes.

chunks.text: The 5th Year M.S. is only available to UC Berkeley EECS and CS undergraduates who apply in their final year. It is a combined Bachelor and Master’s program geared toward highly motivated students who are interested in a professional career.
chunks.question: Who is eligible for the 5th Year M.S. program at Berkeley?
chunks.answer: UC Berkeley EECS and CS undergraduates in final year.

chunks.text: In EE106/206B, Robotic Manipulation and Interaction, students learn about the dynamics and control of groups of robotic manipulators coordinating with each other and interacting with the environment. The class emphasizes design and human-robot interactions, and applications in manufacturing, service robotics, tele-surgery, and locomotion.
chunks.question: What does the EE106/206B class at Berkeley focus on?
chunks.answer: Robotic manipulation, control, and human-robot interactions.

chunks.text: At the northeast corner of the campus, you will find Cory and Soda Halls, homes of the Department of Electrical Engineering and Computer Sciences, with campus dormitories right across the street. Here are some ideas for visiting our department: Sign up for a department tour hosted by the HKN honor society, Sit in on a lower-division EECS class, Meet with an adviser, Join a campus tour sponsored by the University’s visitors center, Attend Cal Day (Spring Only), Attend Community College Day (Fall Only).
chunks.question: Where are Cory and Soda Halls located on campus?
chunks.answer: At the northeast corner of the Berkeley campus.
